questions.question: A hospital is evaluating two patient groups for risk of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA). Group A consists of patients with acute leukemia undergoing chemotherapy, resulting in neutrophil counts <500 cells/μL for over 10 days. Innate immunity components such as alveolar macrophages and neutrophils can clear up to 90% of inhaled Aspergillus spores before they germinate. In some studies, defects in innate immunity have been associated with increased susceptibility to a range of fungal pathogens, not limited to Aspergillus. Group B consists of solid organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressive regimens including high-dose corticosteroids and calcineurin inhibitors, with lymphocyte counts <500 cells/μL but normal neutrophil counts. Patients with lymphoma receiving immunosuppressive therapy have been reported to develop IPA at rates up to 12%, particularly when exposed to environmental sources of Aspergillus. In these cases, both cellular and humoral immune defects may contribute to susceptibility. Both groups are exposed to hospital construction dust containing Aspergillus spores. Among patients undergoing solid organ transplantation, the use of calcineurin inhibitors has been associated with a 12% reduction in T-cell proliferation in vitro. Additionally, certain organ types, such as lung transplants, are linked to higher baseline colonization rates by environmental molds. IPA incidence is 18% in Group A and 5% in Group B. Considering the mechanisms of host defense against Aspergillus, which group is at higher risk for IPA and what is the primary immune defect responsible?

questions.question: A 55-year-old immunocompromised patient with hematologic malignancy develops invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA). Initial therapy with voriconazole (an azole antifungal that inhibits ergosterol synthesis) is started. Micafungin, an echinocandin antifungal, demonstrated a 31% resistance rate among Aspergillus isolates from IPA patients in recent surveillance studies. Its activity profile is positively correlated with other echinocandins but differs from amphotericin B susceptibility patterns. After 7 days, the patient shows persistent fever and worsening lung infiltrates. Azoles, including voriconazole, typically achieve peak plasma concentrations of 2–6 μg/mL after standard dosing in adults. In vitro, azoles demonstrate fungistatic activity against Aspergillus species by inhibiting lanosterol 14-α-demethylase. Fungal cultures reveal Aspergillus fumigatus with a voriconazole minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 8 μg/mL (resistant; susceptible ≤1 μg/mL). The voriconazole therapeutic range for trough concentrations is typically 1–4 μg/mL, and therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended to optimize efficacy and minimize toxicity in patients receiving this agent. In some cases, levels above 4 μg/mL have been associated with increased risk of adverse effects, particularly hepatotoxicity. The patient has mild hepatic dysfunction (elevated liver enzymes), but normal renal function. Echinocandins (which inhibit fungal cell wall β-1,3-glucan synthesis) are available and have no significant hepatic metabolism. Literature suggests that combining voriconazole with echinocandins may have synergistic or additive effects in refractory IPA. Considering the resistance profile, organ function, and drug mechanisms, which antifungal class should be prioritized for monotherapy in this patient?
